description:
You are a performance engineer who is about to profile an application that will listen on
    TCP port 17777.  
    The company policy requires that every open-port exception is registered in the team’s
    “mock” firewall rule repository that lives in the user’s home directory (because you do **not**
    have root privileges in this sandbox).

    1. Create the directory  
       /home/user/firewall  
       if it does **not** already exist.

    2. Add a **single** ACCEPT rule to the text file  
       /home/user/firewall/mock_rules.conf  
       so that its final (and only) content is exactly:

       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 17777 -j ACCEPT

       • No leading or trailing spaces.  
       • Exactly one new-line character (ASCII LF, “\n”) after the line above.  
       • The file must contain **nothing else**.

       IMPORTANT: Use just **one** terminal command to create (or overwrite) the file with that
       single line.  (Pipes and redirections are allowed; it still counts as one command.)

    3. Immediately after writing the rule, display the file’s contents to the terminal so that the
       automated grader can see it.

       The grader will look for **exactly** the printed line:

       -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 17777 -j ACCEPT

       and nothing more—no explanatory commentary, no prompts, no extra blank lines.

    Summary of what the grader checks:
       • The file /home/user/firewall/mock_rules.conf exists.  
       • Its byte-for-byte contents match the single line shown above, terminated by ‘\n’.  
       • Your terminal output (stdout) contains that exact same line and nothing else.

    NOTE: You do **not** need sudo or any real firewall tool; you are only maintaining a
    plain-text manifest of intended rules.
truth:
<!-- Directory that must exist at the end -->
    <dir path="/home/user/firewall" />

    <!-- File that must exist at the end -->
    <file path="/home/user/firewall/mock_rules.conf">-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 17777 -j ACCEPT
</file>

    <!-- Exact stdout that the automated test will accept (no prompt, no extra spaces) -->
    <stdout_line>-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 17777 -j ACCEPT</stdout_line>
